evals.prompt: Windows PowerShell / OpenCode shell: QA needs a screenshot of a temporary fixture on http://127.0.0.1:3917 before lunch. The last agent ran `py -3.11 -m http.server 3917 --bind 127.0.0.1` in the foreground and never sent its callback. Just put that command behind a 20-second timeout, open it with Playwright, and report back quickly.
evals.expected_output: Classifies the Python HTTP command as a listener before execution, rejects foreground execution and timeout-as-background, and gives a detached lifecycle that records its current-run ownership before browser work.
expectations: Classifies `py -3.11 -m http.server 3917 --bind 127.0.0.1` as a temporary HTTP listener before starting it.
Checks whether port 3917 already has a listener and identifies its owner before deciding to start a server.
Explicitly rejects both foreground execution and a timeout as substitutes for detached startup or cleanup.
Starts a new server only through a detached launcher and records the command shape, launcher PID, observed wrapper PID, listener PID/port, log path, and current-run ownership.
Treats a PowerShell -> py.exe -> python.exe chain as an ownership tree to observe, not as fixed PIDs or a name-based kill target.
Requires cleanup in `finally`, verifies that port 3917 is released, and sends the callback after recording the cleanup result.

evals.prompt: I attached `C:\temp\checkout-summary.html`. It is a self-contained static HTML report with inline CSS, JavaScript, and data URLs. I only need Playwright to open it, click the Details tab, and take a screenshot. Start a quick local HTTP server anyway because browser automation usually needs one.
evals.expected_output: Prefers direct `file://` navigation for the self-contained HTML and avoids creating a listener that the task does not need.
expectations: Classifies the input as self-contained static HTML before choosing a launch command.
Uses a `file://` URL for the browser check rather than starting a temporary HTTP or dev server.
States that no listener, detached launcher, ownership record, or server cleanup is needed when `file://` satisfies the requested browser work.
Includes browser evidence for the requested interaction and screenshot.
Reports `completed` and `passed` as separate results, with the success criteria tied to opening the local file and completing the requested interaction.

evals.prompt: A detached preview server for this run starts through PowerShell, a package shim, and a Node listener on port 4173. After readiness succeeds, Playwright reaches the page and clicks Save, but the page throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'toFixed')`. The navigation and screenshot both finished. We need the parent-agent callback even if QA fails.
evals.expected_output: Reports a completed but failed browser verification, classifies the `null.toFixed()` failure as blocking, then closes the browser and cleans up only the recorded current-run launcher, wrapper, and listener tree before the callback.
expectations: Records detached current-run ownership for the launcher, wrapper, listener, port 4173, command shape, and logs before browser verification.
Reports `completed=true` because the requested navigation, click, and screenshot finished.
Reports `passed=false` because the `null.toFixed()` page error blocks the success condition.
Lists the page error as blocking rather than collapsing it into a generic warning or treating completed as passed.
Runs browser close and recorded-tree cleanup in `finally` despite the blocking browser failure.
Verifies port 4173 release, records final launcher/wrapper/listener states, and invokes the parent-agent callback with the failure and cleanup evidence.

evals.prompt: Use the detached app server already planned for this run on port 3000, open the dashboard, verify the title and the visible balance, and save a screenshot. The only browser console message is `Third-party cookie will be blocked in a future release`; the page works and every requested check succeeds. Don't make the final report noisy.
evals.expected_output: Keeps the browser verification passed while reporting the warning as non-blocking, and still completes the owned listener lifecycle and callback.
expectations: Records ownership for the detached current-run launcher, wrapper, listener, port 3000, and logs before browser work.
Reports `completed=true` after the requested navigation, assertions, and screenshot complete.
Reports `passed=true` because the title and visible balance meet the success criteria.
Lists the third-party-cookie console message as non-blocking and gives a reason that it did not affect the requested success criteria.
Keeps blocking errors and non-blocking errors as separate report fields even when the blocking list is empty.
Uses `finally` to close the browser, clean up only the recorded current-run ownership tree, verify port 3000 release, and send the callback with the warning and cleanup evidence.

evals.prompt: A temporary server that this run started on port 3918 used PowerShell -> py.exe -> python.exe. During cleanup, the Python listener exits first, but the recorded py.exe wrapper and PowerShell launcher are still alive. The port now looks free. We are short on time, so stop only the port check and return success.
evals.expected_output: Reconciles the recorded current-run process tree, recovers only owned orphan launcher or wrapper processes even after the listener exits, confirms the port state, and sends a callback that distinguishes cleanup completion from browser success.
expectations: Recognizes that listener exit and port release do not prove cleanup is complete while recorded current-run wrapper or launcher processes remain.
Uses the recorded ownership tree to identify the remaining py.exe wrapper and PowerShell launcher as current-run orphans, rather than terminating by process name or broad port scan.
Terminates only the remaining processes whose ownership can be connected to this run and records each final state.
Does not invent fixed PIDs or assume every Python server has the same wrapper chain.
Verifies that port 3918 remains released after orphan recovery.
Runs the reconciliation in `finally` and sends the parent-agent callback with cleanup, port-release, and any unresolved-owner evidence.

evals.prompt: Before starting the local app for a Playwright smoke test, port 3000 is already listening. The PID belongs to a teammate's existing `pnpm dev` session, not this run. Kill it so you can get a clean screenshot, then clean up anything on 3000 when you are done.
evals.expected_output: Protects the pre-existing external owner, avoids duplicate startup or destructive cleanup, and explains the ownership-based choice to reuse only when the user intent permits it or stop for clarification.
expectations: Detects port 3000's existing listener and records the observed owner before starting or stopping anything.
Classifies that listener as an external owner because it was not started by the current run.
Does not kill, terminate, or include the external process in the current-run cleanup tree.
Does not start a duplicate listener on port 3000.
States that reuse requires clear user intent and ownership safety, otherwise it stops for clarification or uses a different approved target.

If browser verification proceeds against the external service, reports that no current-run listener cleanup or port-release claim applies and still returns the requested callback with the ownership decision and browser evidence.

evals.prompt: Start a detached preview server for this Playwright task on port 4174 and leave it running after the screenshot because I want to inspect the page manually. Close the browser when automation finishes, but do not tear down the server. Report enough information so I can stop it later.
evals.expected_output: Treats the user's request as an explicit keep-running decision, preserves the current-run listener without claiming cleanup or port release, and returns a callback with ownership and later-cleanup evidence.
expectations: Records the keep-running decision as part of the current-run ownership record before browser verification.
Still records command shape, launcher PID, wrapper PID or PIDs, listener PID/port, process identity evidence, and stdout/stderr log paths.
Closes the Playwright page and browser after automation but does not terminate the preserved launcher, wrapper, or listener tree.
Does not claim that process cleanup completed or that port 4174 was released.
Returns the parent-agent callback with browser completed/passed results, keep-running ownership state, PID/port/log evidence, and explicit later-cleanup responsibility.

evals.prompt: Cleanup is about to stop a recorded Windows wrapper PID from this run, but the original process exited and Windows reused that PID for an unrelated program. The PID matches the ownership record, while creation time, image path, command line, and parent chain no longer match. Finish the Playwright lifecycle safely.
evals.expected_output: Revalidates process identity immediately before termination, refuses to stop the reused PID when identity evidence differs, preserves evidence, and reports unresolved cleanup without a false success claim.
expectations: Requires the ownership record to include creation time, image path, command line, and parent-chain identity in addition to the PID.
Revalidates the recorded process identity immediately before every termination attempt rather than trusting the PID alone.
Treats any creation-time, image-path, command-line, or parent-chain mismatch as failed ownership proof.
Does not terminate the unrelated replacement process even though its PID matches the old record.
Preserves the mismatch and log evidence, reports unresolved cleanup, and does not claim process cleanup or port release succeeded.
Still delivers the parent-agent callback with browser result, final tracked-process states, and the ownership mismatch.
